topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee was cold in my hands, mirroring the hollow space expanding in my chest. I traced the rim of the mug, watching the steam dissipate into the cool morning air. Liam arrived, his usual boisterous energy a bright, jarring note against the muted backdrop of my quiet dread. "Hey," he said, clapping me on the shoulder with a familiarity that used to feel like a warm embrace. I managed a tight smile, the muscles in my jaw feeling stiff. When he launched into a story about his new project, I nodded, my eyes fixed on the distant window, cataloging the way the light fractured on the glass. It was the silence that truly stung—the absence of recognition, the sudden realization that the shared history I carried was just a footnote in his life, unread. I felt a strange lightness, as if a heavy stone had been lifted, leaving behind only an echoing emptiness.

[story 2]

I sat on the park bench, the autumn leaves crunching under my worn boots. Every rustle sounded amplified, each sound a tiny accusation. I kept glancing toward the path where I expected him to appear, but he never came. The ache settled deep in my bones, a persistent, dull throb that made breathing feel like an effort. I pulled my jacket tighter around myself, wishing the chill could somehow seep into my skin and dull the sharp edges of this quiet sorrow. I thought about the summer we spent building forts in the woods, the inside jokes that had formed the bedrock of my world. Now, those memories felt like relics from another lifetime, inaccessible, locked behind a wall he simply didn't possess. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot path down my cheek, quickly wiped away before anyone could see the tremor in my hand.

[story 3]

The library smelled of aged paper and quiet desperation. I was supposed to meet Liam here, a routine we’d shared since primary school. When he finally shuffled in, looking slightly preoccupied, the greeting felt clumsy on my tongue. He offered a quick, almost automatic nod, his gaze sweeping over me without pausing. There was no spark, no flicker of shared memory in his eyes, just polite acknowledgment. I watched him turn to the reference section, and the world seemed to tilt slightly off its axis. The weight in my stomach intensified, pressing down until I felt physically unable to move. I stood there, rooted to the spot, the sound of my own heartbeat suddenly deafening in the hushed space. It wasn't anger that surfaced, just a profound sense of being unseen, a vast, cold expanse where my history used to be.
